h1Title: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) is an ascending infection of the female upper genital tract — the uterus, fallopian tubes, ovaries, and contiguous pelvic structures — most commonly initiated by sexually transmitted organisms and unprotected sexual activity. The CDC estimates approximately 750,000 cases of symptomatic PID in US women annually, with rates highest in sexually active women under 25. Many episodes are subclinical, recognised only when infertility, chronic pelvic pain, or an ectopic pregnancy presents. Each episode of PID increases the risk of tubal factor infertility by 10-15%; three episodes confer a tubal occlusion risk of over 50%. Prompt antibiotic therapy against polymicrobial infection limits long-term reproductive harm.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 750,000 symptomatic PID cases annually in the US (CDC); lifetime prevalence in sexually active women estimated at 4-12%
keyStats.demographics: Highest incidence in sexually active women aged 15-25; women with prior PID, multiple sexual partners, or inconsistent condom use are at highest risk; prevalence higher in Black women in US population data
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at first PID episode approximately 20-24 years; rare in post-menopausal women outside of intrauterine procedures
keyStats.globalCases: Global incidence estimated at 1-2% of sexually active women annually in high-income countries; substantially higher in settings with high STI prevalence and limited healthcare access

definition: Pelvic inflammatory disease (ICD-10: N73, N70 for salpingo-oophoritis) is a clinical syndrome of ascending polymicrobial infection involving the endometrium (endometritis), fallopian tubes (salpingitis), ovaries (oophoritis), and potentially the peritoneum, caused by spread of pathogenic microorganisms from the lower genital tract. Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis are the most clinically significant initiating pathogens, but up to 70% of PID cases involve polymicrobial infection including anaerobes, Gardnerella vaginalis, Haemophilus influenzae, Mycoplasma genitalium, and other vaginal flora. The 2021 CDC Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines define PID as a clinical syndrome diagnosed on clinical findings in the absence of another explanation; many episodes are subclinical ('silent PID') and are recognised only retrospectively when reproductive consequences appear. PID represents the most preventable cause of tubal factor infertility and ectopic pregnancy in reproductive-age women. Management is primarily medical with broad-spectrum antibiotics covering all likely pathogens; surgery is reserved for complications such as tubo-ovarian abscess that fails to respond to antibiotics or ruptures.

typesClassification.description: Clinical presentation with pelvic pain (often bilateral), adnexal tenderness, cervical motion tenderness, fever, abnormal discharge, and elevated inflammatory markers. Most episodes are caused by N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis with superimposed polymicrobial infection. Requires prompt antibiotic treatment.

Ascending infection without classic symptoms of pain or fever. Estimated to account for 60-85% of all PID episodes. Detected retrospectively through proximal tubal occlusion on hysterosalpingography, positive Chlamydia serology, or pelvic adhesions found at laparoscopy.

A complex pelvic mass involving the fallopian tube and ovary, filled with purulent material, arising as a complication of acute PID in approximately 15-30% of hospitalised cases. Requires parenteral antibiotics and, if unresponsive, surgical or radiological drainage.

Inflammation of the hepatic capsule and anterior peritoneum from trans-peritoneal spread of N. gonorrhoeae or C. trachomatis. Presents with right upper quadrant pleuritic pain mimicking hepatitis, cholecystitis, or pleuritis; occurs in approximately 10-30% of women with PID.

Persistent or recurrent pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and irregular bleeding from pelvic adhesions, hydrosalpinx, and tubo-ovarian complex following inadequately treated or repeated acute episodes. Managed with pain management, physiotherapy, and surgical adhesiolysis in selected cases.

primarySymptoms: Bilateral lower abdominal and pelvic pain, typically dull, aching, or crampy, often worsening with movement or sexual intercourse; the most common presenting symptom, present in over 90% of clinical PID.
Cervical motion tenderness on bimanual pelvic examination — pain when the examiner moves the cervix from side to side — the classic clinical sign of PID ('chandelier sign').
Adnexal tenderness on palpation of the uterus, fallopian tubes, or ovaries bilaterally or unilaterally, reflecting tubo-ovarian inflammation.
Abnormal vaginal discharge: increased volume, purulent or mucopurulent character, or altered colour compared with the patient's baseline, from endocervical infection.
Fever (temperature ≥38.3°C) present in 30-40% of PID episodes; absence of fever does not exclude PID.
Abnormal uterine bleeding: intermenstrual spotting, post-coital bleeding, or irregular cycles from endometritis disrupting normal endometrial shedding.
Deep dyspareunia — pelvic pain during or after sexual intercourse penetrating deeply — from uterine, tubal, or ovarian tenderness.
Urinary frequency and dysuria from periurethral or bladder inflammation adjacent to infected pelvic structures.
Nausea, vomiting, and anorexia in more severe episodes, particularly when peritoneal irritation or a developing tubo-ovarian abscess is present.
Right upper quadrant pain (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome) from perihepatitis in approximately 10-30% of women with confirmed PID.
earlyWarningSigns: New or worsening pelvic pain in a sexually active woman under 25 not explained by menstruation or a urinary cause — even without discharge or fever — warrants empirical treatment for PID.
Mucopurulent cervicitis on speculum examination (yellow or green endocervical discharge, or easily induced cervical bleeding on swabbing) in a woman with pelvic symptoms.
New onset of post-coital bleeding or intermenstrual spotting alongside pelvic discomfort, suggesting early endometritis.
Onset of pelvic pain within days to weeks of a new sexual partner, gynaecological procedure (IUD insertion, abortion), or unprotected intercourse.
Known contact with a partner diagnosed with gonorrhoea or chlamydia, even in the absence of symptoms — treat empirically per CDC guidelines.
emergencySigns: High fever (≥38.5°C), severe bilateral pelvic pain, vomiting, and a palpable adnexal mass — possible tubo-ovarian abscess requiring hospital admission and parenteral antibiotics.
Acute abdomen with rebound tenderness and guarding from a ruptured tubo-ovarian abscess — a surgical emergency requiring immediate laparotomy or laparoscopic drainage.
Haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) with peritoneal signs in a woman with suspected PID — implies rupture of TOA, ectopic pregnancy, or sepsis; emergency assessment essential.

Suspected ectopic pregnancy (positive pregnancy test, unilateral adnexal tenderness, and pelvic pain) — requires urgent pregnancy test and transvaginal ultrasound to exclude before treating as PID alone.

causes.description: Gonorrhoea infects the endocervix and, if untreated, ascends through the endometrium to the fallopian tubes. N. gonorrhoeae causes a particularly acute and florid salpingitis with rapid tubal damage. Identified in 25-50% of PID cases in settings with high gonorrhoea prevalence; now less common in low-prevalence settings where chlamydia predominates.

C. trachomatis is an intracellular bacterial pathogen that causes indolent ascending infection, often without prominent acute symptoms. It is identified in 40-60% of PID cases in high-income countries. Chlamydial salpingitis causes tubal scarring through both direct invasion and host-immune response to heat-shock protein 60, even when acute symptoms are minimal.

In up to 70% of PID episodes, multiple organisms — anaerobes, Gardnerella vaginalis, Prevotella species, Mycoplasma hominis, Mycoplasma genitalium, enteric Gram-negatives — co-infect the upper genital tract alongside or independently of gonorrhoea and chlamydia. Bacterial vaginosis is associated with a 2-3× elevated risk of PID, partly because BV flora disrupts the cervical mucus barrier.

An emerging sexually transmitted pathogen increasingly recognised in PID, causing salpingitis resistant to azithromycin monotherapy and associated with greater inflammatory damage. Identified in 5-15% of PID cases; diagnosis requires specific NAAT testing. Requires targeted treatment with moxifloxacin when detected.

causes.cause: Iatrogenic introduction of organisms following intrauterine procedures
causes.description: Intrauterine device insertion, therapeutic abortion, hysterosalpingography, hysteroscopy, and endometrial biopsy transiently disrupt the cervical mucus barrier and can introduce vaginal flora into the uterine cavity. PID risk after IUD insertion is highest in the first 3 weeks and in women with STI at the time of insertion.

riskFactors.description: Young women have higher rates of STIs, larger cervical ectopy (exposing more columnar cells susceptible to chlamydia), and a less robust immunological barrier, all of which increase PID risk. Incidence falls sharply after age 25 with acquired immunity and behaviour change.

riskFactors.factor: Multiple sexual partners or new sexual partner
riskFactors.description: Each additional sexual partner increases cumulative STI exposure risk. Having two or more partners in the past 3 months is associated with a 4-5× higher PID risk.
Each episode of PID damages tubal defence mechanisms, making repeat infection and scarring more likely. Prior chlamydia infection increases repeat PID susceptibility 2-3×.
Consistent condom use reduces the risk of gonorrhoea and chlamydia transmission by approximately 85-90% and is the most effective behavioural PID prevention strategy.

BV disrupts normal Lactobacillus-dominant vaginal flora, increasing vaginal pH and reducing mucus defence. BV is associated with a 2-3× higher risk of PID. Treatment of BV with metronidazole reduces this excess risk.
IUD insertion in a woman with untreated gonorrhoea or chlamydia at the time of insertion carries a substantially elevated PID risk. Routine STI testing before insertion largely eliminates this risk.

Vaginal douching disrupts normal flora, increases the risk of BV, and may facilitate ascending infection. Women who douche at least monthly have a 1.8-3.4× higher risk of PID compared with non-douchers.

affectedDemographics: Sexually active women aged 15-25 bear the highest burden; rates fall markedly after age 30 with immunity acquisition and behavioural change.
Black women in US studies have 2-3× higher PID rates than white women, partly attributed to higher gonorrhoea and chlamydia prevalence and healthcare access disparities.

Women with lower socioeconomic status and limited healthcare access are disproportionately affected by PID and its long-term reproductive consequences due to delayed diagnosis and incomplete treatment.

Women in settings with high STI prevalence and limited sexual health services have substantially higher PID rates; in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, PID and its complications are a major cause of tubal factor infertility.
diagnosisOverview: PID is primarily a clinical diagnosis; waiting for microbiological confirmation risks treatment delay and irreversible tubal damage. The 2021 CDC guidelines recommend empirical treatment for PID in any sexually active woman with pelvic or lower abdominal pain who has at least one of the following on examination: cervical motion tenderness, uterine tenderness, or adnexal tenderness — provided no other cause is identified. The clinical threshold is intentionally low because missing PID is more harmful than over-treating an uncertain case. Additional criteria supporting the diagnosis include fever ≥38.3°C, cervical mucopurulent discharge or friability, elevated white cell count or CRP/ESR, laboratory confirmation of gonorrhoea or chlamydia, and endometrial biopsy showing endometritis (histological criterion used in research). Endocervical swabs for gonorrhoea culture and sensitivity, chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT, Mycoplasma genitalium NAAT, and vaginal wet mount (for BV and trichomonas) are sent at presentation. Pregnancy test is mandatory to exclude ectopic pregnancy. Transvaginal ultrasound is used when a pelvic mass suggesting tubo-ovarian abscess is suspected; it also excludes ovarian cyst torsion. Laparoscopy is the gold standard for diagnosing salpingitis with direct visualisation of inflamed tubes and exudate, but is reserved for cases where the diagnosis is uncertain or there is no response to treatment, as most PID is managed on clinical grounds without laparoscopy.

diagnosticTests.test: Endocervical NAAT for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the most common causative organisms; guides antibiotic selection and partner notification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab is taken from the endocervix during speculum examination or a first-catch urine sample is used for NAAT testing. Results return in 24-48 hours. A negative result does not exclude PID — empirical treatment should not await results in symptomatic women.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies tubo-ovarian abscess, free fluid in the pelvis, and thickened or fluid-filled fallopian tubes (hydrosalpinx or pyosalpinx); excludes ovarian torsion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small ultrasound probe placed in the vagina; 15-20 minutes, mildly uncomfortable. TOA appears as a complex adnexal mass with mixed echogenicity. Absence of ultrasound findings does not exclude PID.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count (FBC), CRP, and ESR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated WBC, CRP, and ESR support the diagnosis and assess severity; used to monitor treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. CRP above 30 mg/L and WBC above 10×10⁹/L are common in acute PID. Normal inflammatory markers do not exclude PID — 50% of cases have normal WBC.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine pregnancy test (serum beta-hCG if test positive)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory to exclude ectopic pregnancy, which can present identically to PID and is a surgical emergency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard urine dipstick test or blood draw. A positive result requires urgent transvaginal ultrasound to determine intrauterine or ectopic location. Ectopic pregnancy and PID can coexist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses bacterial vaginosis (clue cells, fishy amine odour) and trichomonas, both associated with PID risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A vaginal swab is prepared as a wet slide viewed under the microscope immediately. Takes 5-10 minutes. BV is identified in up to 60% of women with PID.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard for direct visualisation of salpingitis and tubo-ovarian involvement; used when diagnosis is uncertain or there is no response to 72 hours of antibiotics
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A surgical procedure under general anaesthesia with 2-3 small abdominal incisions. A camera confirms inflamed tubes, tubo-ovarian adhesions, and free purulent peritoneal fluid. May be combined with lysis of adhesions or drainage of TOA.

treatmentOverview: Prompt empirical antibiotic treatment is the cornerstone of PID management and should be initiated as soon as a clinical diagnosis is made, before microbiological results are available. The 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines recommend broad-spectrum regimens covering N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis, anaerobes, and Gram-negative enteric organisms. For outpatient management of mild-to-moderate PID, the recommended regimen is a single dose of intramuscular ceftriaxone 500 mg plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days plus oral metronidazole 500 mg twice daily for 14 days. Inpatient parenteral treatment is indicated for: tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, inability to tolerate oral medication, failure of outpatient treatment, or surgical emergency exclusion required. Parenteral regimens include IV cefoxitin or cefotetan plus IV/oral doxycycline, or IV clindamycin plus IV gentamicin. Mycoplasma genitalium-positive PID requires moxifloxacin 400 mg daily for 14 days. All patients should be re-evaluated at 72 hours to confirm clinical improvement; absence of improvement mandates hospitalisation and parenteral therapy. Partner treatment is essential: all sexual partners from the prior 60 days should be tested and treated empirically for gonorrhoea and chlamydia. Tubo-ovarian abscess not responding to 72 hours of intravenous antibiotics requires image-guided drainage (transvaginal or CT-guided) or surgical drainage. Ruptured TOA is a surgical emergency requiring laparoscopy or laparotomy with drainage and possible salpingo-oophorectomy. The ACOG and CDC recommend follow-up at 4-6 weeks after completing treatment to confirm resolution.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose + doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily × 14 days + metronidazole 500 mg orally twice daily × 14 days (CDC 2021 outpatient regimen)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line outpatient regimen for mild-to-moderate PID in non-pregnant women. Ceftriaxone covers gonorrhoea; doxycycline covers chlamydia and Mycoplasma hominis; metronidazole covers anaerobes and BV-associated organisms. Full 14-day course is required even if symptoms resolve early.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in 75-90% of outpatient cases; failure to improve in 72 hours requires hospitalisation and parenteral therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Cefoxitin 2 g IV q6h + doxycycline 100 mg IV/orally q12h (CDC 2021 inpatient regimen A)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line parenteral regimen for moderate-severe PID or tubo-ovarian abscess. IV therapy continues until clinical improvement (≥24 hours after defervescence and pain reduction), then transitions to oral doxycycline 100 mg plus metronidazole 500 mg to complete 14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in 85-95% of hospitalised cases; TOA resolution rate 70-80% with antibiotics alone

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h + gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily (CDC 2021 inpatient regimen B)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative parenteral regimen; preferred when aminoglycoside toxicity is not a concern and polymicrobial anaerobic infection is prominent. Transition to oral clindamycin 450 mg five times daily or oral doxycycline after clinical improvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure equivalent to cefoxitin-doxycycline regimen in randomised trials; gentamicin requires renal function monitoring

medicalTreatments.name: Moxifloxacin 400 mg orally once daily × 14 days
medicalTreatments.description: Treatment of choice for Mycoplasma genitalium-positive PID; M. genitalium is frequently azithromycin-resistant. Should replace doxycycline in regimens when M. genitalium is confirmed on NAAT testing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eradication rates for M. genitalium above 85% in treated cases; superior to doxycycline and azithromycin for this pathogen

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole 500 mg orally twice daily × 14 days (component of all outpatient regimens)
medicalTreatments.description: Essential for anaerobic coverage in polymicrobial PID; also treats coexisting bacterial vaginosis, reducing relapse risk. Common side effects include nausea, metallic taste, and alcohol intolerance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significantly reduces treatment failure when added to cephalosporin-doxycycline regimens for outpatient PID

surgicalOptions.name: Transvaginal or CT-guided abscess drainage
surgicalOptions.description: Image-guided needle drainage of a tubo-ovarian abscess that fails to respond to 72 hours of intravenous antibiotics. Performed by interventional radiology or a gynaecologist with an ultrasound probe. Avoids laparotomy in most patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 75-85% success in preventing laparotomy in patients with TOA >6 cm unresponsive to antibiotics; recurrence requiring repeat drainage in 15-20%

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic drainage and lysis of adhesions
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive surgical drainage of tubo-ovarian abscess, removal of purulent material, and division of acute pelvic adhesions under direct vision. Preferred over open laparotomy when laparoscopic access is feasible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Over 90% clinical cure for TOA drainage; recurrence is rare after surgical drainage; fertility outcomes better than open surgery

surgicalOptions.name: Laparotomy with salpingo-oophorectomy or total abdominal hysterectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for ruptured TOA with haemodynamic instability or when laparoscopic access is impossible. May require removal of one or both tubes and ovaries. Fertility-preserving approaches are attempted in women who have not completed childbearing.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive treatment of ruptured TOA; high perioperative success; significant fertility impact from removal of reproductive organs

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical vaginal probiotics (Lactobacillus-based)
alternativeTreatments.description: Restoration of Lactobacillus-dominant vaginal flora may reduce BV recurrence and theoretically lower PID risk. Small trials suggest benefit for BV prevention; no direct RCT evidence for PID prevention as yet.

whySeeSpecialist: An obstetrician-gynaecologist or sexual health physician should manage any woman with suspected PID. Early empirical treatment limits long-term damage; delay of even 1-2 days significantly increases the risk of tubal infertility. Women with recurrent PID, TOA, or infertility following PID should be referred to a reproductive endocrinology specialist.
doctorSelectionGuide: Any obstetrician-gynaecologist or sexual health clinic can appropriately manage uncomplicated PID. For TOA requiring drainage, choose a centre with interventional radiology and gynaecologic surgery available around the clock. For women with PID-related infertility, a reproductive endocrinology and infertility subspecialist with experience in tubal disease should guide fertility evaluation and treatment planning.
hospitalCriteria: 24-hour gynaecological surgery capability for ruptured TOA emergencies
Interventional radiology for image-guided TOA drainage
NAAT testing for gonorrhoea, chlamydia, and Mycoplasma genitalium available in-house or with rapid turnaround
Transvaginal ultrasound available for adnexal mass assessment
Sexual health and contact-tracing service for partner notification and treatment
keyFacilities: Sexual health clinics with same-day NAAT and empirical treatment protocols
Gynaecology emergency units with immediate ultrasound access
Reproductive medicine centres for PID-related infertility assessment
preventionStrategies: Use condoms consistently with every sexual encounter — 85-90% effective at preventing gonorrhoea and chlamydia transmission, the leading PID initiators.
Annual chlamydia and gonorrhoea screening for all sexually active women under 25 and for older women with new or multiple partners — per CDC and USPSTF Grade B recommendation.
Screen for and treat bacterial vaginosis, which increases PID risk 2-3×; avoid douching, which disrupts normal vaginal flora.
Test for and treat STIs before intrauterine procedures (IUD insertion, hysteroscopy, therapeutic abortion) to eliminate procedure-associated PID risk.
Reduce number of concurrent sexual partners and ensure mutual STI testing before unprotected sex within new relationships.
Treat sexual partners empirically for gonorrhoea and chlamydia when PID is diagnosed, to prevent re-infection (expedited partner therapy where legally permitted).
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full 14-day antibiotic course even if symptoms improve early — incomplete treatment leaves residual infection that causes tubal scarring.
Abstain from sexual intercourse until the full treatment course is completed and sexual partners have also been treated.
Attend the 72-hour follow-up appointment — clinical assessment at this point is required to confirm improvement and determine if inpatient escalation is needed.
Avoid vaginal douching permanently — it disrupts normal protective flora and increases recurrent BV and PID risk.
Follow up at 4-6 weeks after completing antibiotics to confirm full resolution and reassess STI risk.
Discuss long-acting reversible contraception (IUD is safe after STI clearance is confirmed) or condom use with your provider to reduce future PID risk.

recommended: Adequate protein and caloric intake during antibiotic treatment to support immune recovery
Probiotic-containing foods (live yogurt, kefir) or oral probiotics (Lactobacillus rhamnosus, L. reuteri) during and after antibiotic courses to restore vaginal and gut flora
Adequate fluid intake (>2 L/day) to support antibiotic clearance and urinary tract health
Vitamin C-rich foods to support immune function during acute infection
avoid: Alcohol during metronidazole treatment — causes a disulfiram-like reaction with severe nausea, flushing, and vomiting
Dairy products close in time to doxycycline doses — calcium reduces doxycycline absorption; take 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals
Douching or vaginal deodorants, which disrupt protective flora and slow recovery
High-sugar diet, which may promote yeast overgrowth during antibiotic-induced flora disruption

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the acute phase of PID while febrile and in significant pain. Light walking is appropriate once fever has resolved and pain is manageable. Strenuous exercise, sexual intercourse, and activities that increase intra-abdominal pressure should be avoided until the antibiotic course is completed and symptoms have fully resolved. After complete recovery, return to normal activity is unrestricted.
dailyManagement: Take doxycycline with food and a full glass of water to reduce oesophageal irritation; avoid lying down for 30 minutes after each dose.
Take metronidazole with food to reduce nausea; avoid alcohol strictly during and for 48 hours after completing the course.
Monitor temperature twice daily during the first 72 hours; report persistent fever above 38°C or worsening pain to your clinician.
Track vaginal discharge character and volume — worsening or failure to improve by 72 hours warrants re-evaluation.
Notify all sexual partners from the past 60 days so they can be tested and treated; this is essential to prevent re-infection.
Attend follow-up appointment at 72 hours and again at 4-6 weeks after completing treatment.
prognosis: With prompt and complete antibiotic treatment, most women with acute PID recover fully without long-term reproductive sequelae. The major determinants of outcome are time to treatment, completeness of antibiotic coverage, and number of prior PID episodes. Each episode of PID carries a 10-15% risk of tubal occlusion; after three episodes the risk exceeds 50%. Ectopic pregnancy risk increases 6-10-fold after a single PID episode compared with unaffected women. Chronic pelvic pain following PID affects approximately 18% of women and is caused by peritubal and peritoneal adhesions. Tubo-ovarian abscess treated with antibiotics and drainage resolves without sequelae in 75-85% of cases; recurrence requiring surgery is rare after adequate drainage. Fertility outcomes following PID are directly related to the severity and number of episodes — a single mild treated episode has minimal long-term fertility impact, while recurrent or severe episodes cause clinically significant tubal factor infertility in 30-50% of affected women.
recoveryTimeline: Most women with uncomplicated outpatient PID experience pain improvement within 48-72 hours of starting antibiotics; full symptom resolution by day 7-10. The full 14-day antibiotic course is required regardless of symptom timing. Hospitalised patients typically defervesce and have significant pain reduction within 48-72 hours; transition to oral antibiotics occurs at that point. TOA resolution on ultrasound occurs over 4-8 weeks.
complications: Tubal factor infertility: tubal scarring and occlusion from salpingitis impairs natural conception; 10-15% per episode of PID (Westrom et al. JAMA 1992); managed by tubal surgery or IVF.

Ectopic pregnancy: damaged tubes increase the risk of implantation in the tube rather than the uterus; women with prior PID have a 6-10× higher ectopic risk; always excluded with pregnancy test and ultrasound in subsequent pregnancies.
Chronic pelvic pain: pelvic adhesions and hydrosalpinx cause persistent pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and reduced quality of life in approximately 18% of women after PID.
Tubo-ovarian abscess: develops in 15-30% of women hospitalised with acute PID; requires parenteral antibiotics and often image-guided or surgical drainage.

Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis): right upper quadrant pain from hepatic capsule inflammation; resolves with treatment of the underlying PID but can be misdiagnosed as biliary or hepatic disease.
Recurrent PID: each subsequent episode multiplies tubal and ovarian damage; three or more episodes produce severe pelvic adhesions, ovarian reserve reduction, and often permanent infertility.

supportResources.name: CDC — Pelvic Inflammatory Disease Treatment Guidelines 2021
supportResources.description: Official US treatment guidelines for PID including drug regimens, hospitalisation criteria, and partner management from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: ACOG — Pelvic Inflammatory Disease Patient FAQ
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on PID diagnosis, treatment, and prevention from the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists.

supportResources.name: Planned Parenthood — PID
supportResources.description: Plain-language patient education on PID symptoms, testing, treatment, and how it relates to STI prevention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Endometriosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Endometriosis and PID both cause chronic pelvic pain and dyspareunia. Endometriosis is a non-infectious inflammatory condition related to ectopic endometrial tissue, characterised by cyclical pain and diagnosed by laparoscopy. PID is an acute infection with fever, discharge, and cervical motion tenderness. Endometriosis does not respond to antibiotics; PID does not respond to hormonal suppression.

confusedWithConditions.name: Uterine Fibroids
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibroids cause heavy menstrual bleeding and pelvic pressure from benign uterine tumours. PID causes acute pelvic pain, fever, and discharge from genital tract infection. Fibroids are not infectious and do not cause cervical motion tenderness; PID does. Ultrasound reliably distinguishes them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Urinary Tract Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A UTI causes dysuria, frequency, and suprapubic discomfort without adnexal or cervical tenderness, fever above 38.5°C, or vaginal discharge. PID involves bilateral pelvic pain and cervical motion tenderness, with mucopurulent discharge. Urine microscopy and culture distinguish UTI from PID; both can coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Uterine Fibroids
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions cause pelvic pain in women of reproductive age; they may coexist and are managed by the same specialty.

relatedConditions.name: Endometriosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential diagnosis for chronic pelvic pain after PID; both cause pelvic adhesions and fertility impairment, sometimes requiring laparoscopic evaluation to distinguish.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Hormonal irregularities in PCOS can cause pelvic pain and abnormal bleeding overlapping with PID presentation; both affect reproductive-age women and require gynaecological management.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic PID with abnormal bleeding can contribute to iron-deficiency anaemia; anaemia worsens recovery from acute infection.

faqs.question: What is pelvic inflammatory disease?
faqs.answer: Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) is an infection of the female upper reproductive tract — the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries — most often caused by sexually transmitted bacteria including Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. It affects approximately 750,000 US women annually and is the most preventable cause of tubal infertility.

faqs.question: What causes pelvic inflammatory disease?
faqs.answer: PID is caused by bacteria ascending from the vagina and cervix into the uterus and fallopian tubes. Gonorrhoea and chlamydia are the most common initiating pathogens. Bacterial vaginosis flora, Mycoplasma genitalium, and other vaginal organisms frequently co-infect. Risk increases with multiple partners, unprotected sex, and intrauterine procedures.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of PID?
faqs.answer: The most common symptoms are bilateral lower pelvic pain, abnormal vaginal discharge, and pain during sex. Fever above 38°C, irregular bleeding, and painful pelvic examination are also common. Up to 60-85% of PID episodes cause no or minimal symptoms and are only recognised when infertility or ectopic pregnancy occurs.

faqs.question: How is PID treated?
faqs.answer: PID is treated with antibiotics covering gonorrhoea, chlamydia, anaerobes, and other polymicrobial pathogens for 14 days. The standard CDC outpatient regimen is intramuscular ceftriaxone plus oral doxycycline and metronidazole. Severe PID or tubo-ovarian abscess requires hospitalisation and intravenous antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can PID cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Each episode of PID carries a 10-15% risk of tubal scarring and occlusion that impairs fertility. After three episodes, the risk of tubal factor infertility exceeds 50%. IVF or tubal surgery can help women with PID-related infertility conceive.

faqs.question: How long does PID take to treat?
faqs.answer: Antibiotic treatment lasts 14 days. Most women notice symptom improvement within 48-72 hours. The full course must be completed even when feeling better, as incomplete treatment causes tubal scarring. A follow-up visit at 72 hours confirms clinical improvement and informs whether inpatient escalation is needed.

faqs.question: Can PID come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. PID can recur with re-exposure to untreated partners or new STI acquisition. Recurrent PID multiplies tubal damage significantly. Ensuring partner treatment, consistent condom use, and regular STI screening reduces recurrence risk substantially.

faqs.question: Does PID increase the risk of ectopic pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tubal scarring from PID increases the risk of an ectopic pregnancy 6-10-fold compared with unaffected women. Any positive pregnancy test in a woman with prior PID requires prompt transvaginal ultrasound to confirm intrauterine location.

faqs.question: Can PID be diagnosed without symptoms?
faqs.answer: Silent PID — without pain, fever, or discharge — is estimated to account for 60-85% of all episodes. It is identified retrospectively through blocked tubes on fertility investigation, positive chlamydia serology, or pelvic adhesions at laparoscopy. Routine STI screening catches the infections that cause silent PID before tubal damage occurs.

faqs.question: What is a tubo-ovarian abscess?
faqs.answer: A tubo-ovarian abscess is a pus-filled mass involving the fallopian tube and ovary that develops as a complication of severe PID. It presents with fever, severe pelvic pain, and a palpable adnexal mass. Treatment requires intravenous antibiotics; abscesses larger than 6 cm unresponsive to antibiotics need image-guided or surgical drainage.

faqs.question: Does my partner need treatment for PID?
faqs.answer: Yes. All sexual partners from the past 60 days must be tested and treated empirically for gonorrhoea and chlamydia, even if they have no symptoms. Untreated partners will re-infect the patient. Expedited partner therapy — providing antibiotic prescriptions for the partner without requiring a clinic visit — is recommended by CDC where legally permitted.

faqs.question: Can I get PID from an IUD?
faqs.answer: The risk of PID from IUD insertion is highest in the first 3 weeks after placement and mainly affects women with untreated gonorrhoea or chlamydia at the time of insertion. Routine STI testing before IUD insertion eliminates most of this risk. Long-term IUD use in women without STIs does not increase PID risk.

faqs.question: Is PID the same as an STI?
faqs.answer: PID is a consequence of untreated or inadequately treated sexually transmitted infections, not an STI itself. Gonorrhoea and chlamydia are the most common STIs that trigger PID when left untreated. Not all PID is caused by STIs — vaginal flora organisms and iatrogenic introduction of bacteria after gynaecological procedures can also cause PID.

faqs.question: How can I prevent PID?
faqs.answer: Consistent condom use reduces STI transmission by 85-90%. Annual chlamydia and gonorrhoea screening for all sexually active women under 25 is recommended by the CDC. Avoiding douching preserves protective vaginal flora. Treating bacterial vaginosis and getting tested before new sexual relationships are also effective prevention strategies.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PID and endometriosis?
faqs.answer: PID is an acute infection causing fever, abnormal discharge, and cervical motion tenderness, treated with antibiotics. Endometriosis is a non-infectious inflammatory condition causing cyclical pelvic pain and infertility from ectopic endometrial tissue, managed with hormonal therapy or laparoscopic surgery. Both can cause chronic pelvic pain but require completely different treatments.

metaTitle: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: PID (pelvic inflammatory disease) affects 750,000 US women yearly. Symptoms, antibiotic regimens, tubo-ovarian abscess management and fertility impact reviewed.
